one_line_takeaway: DMark is the first dLLM watermarking scheme, using logit-predicted context and forward+backward green lists to recover 92-99.5% detection at 1% FPR where naive AR-watermark adaptation manages only ~50-71%, though paraphrasing still evades it.
key_finding: Predictive-Bidirectional watermarking achieves near-perfect detection of 92.0-99.5% TPR at 1% FPR across the three dLLMs and two datasets, versus 49.6-71.2% for the naive direct KGW adaptation, 54.2-79.3% for predictive alone, and 74.2-88.0% for bidirectional alone. It is also markedly more robust: ~95-97% TPR at 1% FPR under 10% token-level edits and 83-88% even under 20% edits, where KGW collapses to 26-35%. The paper recommends gamma=0.5, delta=2.0 as the practical deployment configuration, giving >92% detection while keeping perplexity readable.
core_contribution: DMark, presented as the first watermarking framework designed specifically for diffusion large language models (dLLMs). Because dLLMs finalize tokens in arbitrary order via iterative denoising rather than left-to-right, the sequential-context assumption of green/red-list watermarks (e.g., KGW) breaks. DMark restores detectability with three of its own strategies: (1) predictive watermarking, which predicts a missing neighbor token from the model's parallel logits and uses it to construct the green list; (2) bidirectional watermarking, which introduces a backward green list G'_i (tokens at position i that make the subsequent token x_{i+1} green) so a watermark bias can be applied using either the preceding or the following neighbor; and (3) predictive-bidirectional watermarking, which combines both so a bias is applied at every position regardless of generation order. The paper also formalizes the watermarking problem for dLLMs and explains why AR watermarks fail on them.
threat_model: Content-provenance / attribution setting: a defender embeds a key-seeded statistical signal into dLLM-generated text so that AI-generated content can later be detected (deterring plagiarism, enabling responsible disclosure) with a low false-positive rate against human text. The adversary tries to remove or weaken the watermark while preserving text utility, via token-level edits (deletion, insertion, swap, substitution at 10-20% rates) and paraphrasing (GPT-5-nano prompt-based and Dipper). Detection is a z-score test over the green-token proportion, thresholded at fixed low FPRs (0.5%, 1%, 5%).
limitations: Paraphrasing remains the main weakness (acknowledged as a known limit of LLM watermarking generally): Dipper at 20% diversity cuts Predictive-Bidirectional to 72.6% TPR@1%FPR and GPT-5-nano paraphrasing to only 51.2%. Strong watermarking (delta>=5.0) gives perfect detection but degrades quality sharply (PPL exceeds 20 on ELI5 at gamma=0.25), forcing a detectability-quality trade-off; short texts (L<50) need large delta to be reliably detected. Evaluation is limited to three 7-8B open-source dLLMs and two datasets with low-confidence remasking; commercial dLLMs (Mercury, Gemini Diffusion) are not tested. The 'KGW' comparison is the authors' own naive adaptation, not an independent competing dLLM watermark, since the paper claims to be first in this space; predictive and bidirectional are internal ablations.

quantitative_results: Predictive-Bidirectional: 92.0-99.5% TPR @1% FPR across all models/datasets (min 92.0% LLaDA-Instruct-8B C4, max 99.5% Dream-v0 ELI5)
Direct KGW adaptation: 49.6-71.2% TPR @1% FPR (min 49.6% LLaDA-Instruct-8B C4, max 71.2% LLaDA-1.5-8B C4)
Direct KGW: only ~67% of tokens had available preceding context (LLaDA on ELI5), causing weak signal
Robustness under 10% token edits: PBidir ~95-97% TPR@1%FPR vs KGW 45-53%
Robustness under 20% token edits: PBidir 83-88% TPR@1%FPR vs KGW 26-35%
Paraphrasing: Dipper (20% diversity) 72.6% TPR@1%FPR; GPT-5-nano 51.2% TPR@1%FPR for PBidir
Parameter sensitivity (PBidir, LLaDA-Instruct, n=200): gamma=0.25/delta=2.0 -> 96.6% TPR on C4; gamma=0.75/delta=2.0 -> 60.6% on C4; PPL <6.5 at delta=2.0, exceeds 20 on ELI5 at delta=5.0/gamma=0.25
Recommended config gamma=0.5, delta=2.0 gives >92% detection with readable quality
Watermarked examples: z-scores 6.37-9.33 vs near-zero unwatermarked; PPL rises modestly from 2.32-3.75 to 3.43-6.01
baselines_compared: Direct adaptation of KGW (Kirchenbauer et al. 2023) to dLLMs -- the authors' own naive adaptation of an AR watermark, used as a lower-bound/strawman since no prior dLLM watermark exists (paper claims to be first)
